ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a white bread entry; this record is a prepared dish with added butter and sugar, so the match is approximate rather than exact, hence 'closest_parent'.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — white bread with added fat and sugar (approximate FoodEx2 anchor for refined wheat bread preparation)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for white bread with butter and sugar as a composite dish; A000E (bread and similar products) is used as the closest defensible anchor, hence 'closest_parent'.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite bread preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is two slices of white bread (approximately 60 g) spread with butter and sprinkled with white sugar, commonly eaten at breakfast in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with butter and sugar — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for white bread with butter and sugar (cr_id F05020120). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from butter content (~8.5 g/100g saturated fat based on typical butter composition at ~14 g total fat from butter); monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are set to -1 as HPB does not report detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 70 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for white bread (GI ~70, glucose reference). The addition of butter slightly lowers the effective GI due to fat slowing gastric emptying, but the added sugar (15.5 g/100g) offsets this benefit. GI 70 is used as a conservative estimate for this composite preparation. Glycemic load per 60 g serving calculated as: (70 × 49.35 × 0.60) / 100 = 20.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (15.5 g/100g) with negligible potassium (4 mg/100g), elevated sodium (314 mg/100g), and high saturated fat — multiple independent blood pressure risk factors
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this preparation entirely; the combination of high sugar driving insulin-mediated sodium retention, very low potassium, and elevated sodium creates compounding cardiovascular risk — substitute with wholegrain bread and an unsalted nut butter spread.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI white bread base (GI 70) combined with 15.5 g added sugar/100g and only 1.79 g dietary fibre/100g — severe postprandial glucose spike risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this food; the combination of refined white bread, added sugar exceeding 15 g/100g, and minimal fibre (1.79 g/100g) produces rapid and pronounced postprandial hyperglycaemia — replace with wholegrain or seeded bread with a low-sugar spread and no added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g from butter), trace trans fat (0.1 g/100g), high added sugar (15.5 g/100g), and elevated sodium (314 mg/100g) — atherogenic and pro-inflammatory nutrient profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this preparation; the saturated fat from butter exceeds recommended limits per serving, trans fat is present, added sugar promotes dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation, and sodium contributes to hypertensive cardiovascular load — choose wholegrain bread with extra-virgin olive oil instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g), trace trans fat (0.1 g/100g), and high refined sugar (15.5 g/100g) — strongly pro-inflammatory combination with no anti-inflammatory factors
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this preparation; butter-derived saturated fat above 5 g/100g, any trans fat, and high added sugar collectively promote inflammatory mediator production — replace with wholegrain bread, extra-virgin olive oil, and a small portion of fresh fruit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with butter and sugar is a simple, widely consumed breakfast preparation in Singapore and Malaysia, consisting of sliced refined white bread spread with butter and sprinkled with white granulated sugar. The texture is soft and pillowy from the white bread, enriched with the creamy, fatty mouthfeel of butter, and sweetened by the granulated sugar that partially dissolves into the spread. It is a common kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) breakfast item, often paired with kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea). Nutritionally, this preparation is energy-dense at 345 kcal per 100 g, with 49.35 g carbohydrate, 14.17 g fat (predominantly saturated from butter at approximately 8.5 g/100g), 6.94 g protein, and 15.5 g sugar per 100 g. Dietary fibre is low at 1.79 g/100g. Sodium is elevated at 314 mg/100g. Potassium is negligible at 4 mg/100g. The glycemic index is high (~70), and the glycemic load per standard 60 g serving is approximately 20.7 g. This food is clinically high-risk for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its combination of high added sugar, high saturated fat, trace trans fat, elevated sodium, and negligible fibre and potassium. It is not suitable for any of these conditions and should be replaced with wholegrain alternatives.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020120) with confidence 0.95. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. Saturated fat is estimated from known butter composition at the reported total fat level; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat are not reported by HPB for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white bread.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite white bread with butter and sugar preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond calcium, iron, and potassium not reported in the HPB source for this entry. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on known trace trans fat content of butter (~0.1 g/100g of composite dish); this is a conservative estimate consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for butter. saturated_fat_g of 8.5 g/100g is estimated from the butter contribution to total fat (14.17 g/100g total fat, with butter-derived fat being predominantly saturated); this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for butter composition. All core macros (protein_g 6.94, fat_g 14.17, carbohydrate_g 49.35) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white bread; the addition of butter and sugar does not materially change the GI classification from high. Glycemic load per 60 g serving = (70 × 49.35 × 0.60) / 100 = 20.7 g.
